content.text: Every interaction with Claude begins with a conversation. Understanding how to start a new conversation — and the choices available at that moment — sets up the rest of the interaction for success. The new chat interface in Claude.ai provides model selection, project context, and conversation settings, all accessible before the first message is sent.
The Claude.ai Interface at a Glance

Claude.ai is the web application at claude.ai that provides access to Claude without any coding or API setup. The interface consists of a left sidebar and a main conversation area. The sidebar contains navigation for conversations, projects, and account settings. The main area shows the active conversation or a new chat start screen when no conversation is selected.

The interface design follows a standard chat application layout that most users will find familiar: past conversations are listed on the left, the current conversation occupies the center, and input is at the bottom. The design is intentionally minimal, prioritizing the conversation content over chrome and controls.

attrs.alt: Claude.ai interface overview and key elements

content.text: Finding the New Chat Button
On Desktop (claude.ai in a browser)
On the desktop web interface, new chat is initiated in one of several ways:
New chat button
— appears as a pencil icon or 'New chat' text at the top of the left sidebar, typically near the Claude logo or above the conversation list.
Keyboard shortcut
— pressing Shift+Option+N (Mac) or Shift+Alt+N (Windows/Linux) opens a new chat in most browser versions.
Clicking empty space
— clicking outside any existing conversation in the sidebar, or clicking Claude's logo, often navigates to the new chat start screen.
On Mobile (claude.ai app)
In the Claude.ai mobile app, new conversations are started by:
Compose button
— a pencil or compose icon, typically in the bottom right or top right of the screen
From the sidebar
— swipe right or tap a menu icon to open the sidebar, then tap New chat
From the home screen
— the app may open directly to a new chat prompt when launched

attrs.alt: Workflow for starting a new conversation

content.text: The New Chat Start Screen

When you initiate a new chat, the main area typically shows a blank conversation area with a message input box at the bottom. Depending on your plan and interface version, you may also see suggested prompts, recent files, or quick-action buttons. The page is minimal because the most important interaction is your first message.

Above the message input, you may see a model selector or project indicator. These allow you to choose which Claude model will power the conversation and whether the conversation belongs to a project context.
Choosing a Model
Available Models

Claude.ai offers access to one or more Claude models, depending on your plan. As of mid-2025, the commonly available models include Claude 3.5 Sonnet (balanced intelligence and speed), Claude 3.7 Sonnet (expanded capabilities including extended thinking), and occasionally others. The model selector appears in the interface when multiple models are available to your account.
Model
Strengths
Use Cases
Claude 3.5 Sonnet
Fast, capable, versatile
Most tasks; good default
Claude 3.7 Sonnet
Extended thinking, complex reasoning
Hard problems, analysis, code
Claude 3 Haiku
Very fast, lower cost
Simple queries, high-volume tasks (API)
Claude 3 Opus
Maximum intelligence
Most demanding tasks (API)
Selecting a Model

Click the model selector (usually a dropdown or a button showing the current model name) and choose from the available options. The choice applies to the current conversation. If you want to compare responses from different models to the same prompt, start separate conversations with each model.

For most users on Claude.ai Pro, the default model (usually Claude 3.5 or 3.7 Sonnet) is the right choice for the majority of tasks. Model selection matters most when you have a specific reason to use a different model — extended thinking for a difficult problem, or a faster model for many quick queries.
Starting a Conversation Inside vs. Outside a Project
Outside a Project (Standard Chat)

A conversation started outside any project is a standalone chat. It has no project instructions and no project knowledge files. Claude starts with only its training knowledge and whatever you provide in the conversation. Standalone chats are appropriate for one-off tasks, exploratory conversations, and tasks that don't require consistent context across sessions.
Inside a Project

A conversation started inside a project inherits the project's instructions and knowledge files automatically. Navigate to the project in the sidebar, then click New chat within the project. This is the right choice for recurring tasks where you have set up project context — coding work in a project with your team's conventions, writing work in a project with your style guide, research in a project with your background documents.

The distinction between starting inside and outside a project is one of the most important choices at the start of a conversation. If you have a relevant project set up, always start inside it — the context difference directly affects response quality for domain-specific tasks.
Writing Your First Message
What Makes a Good Opening Message

The opening message sets the direction for the entire conversation. A good opening message tells Claude: what you want, any relevant context, the format you prefer for the response, and any constraints it should apply.

# Weak opening (too vague)
"Help me with this code."

# Better opening (specific, contextual)
"I have a Python function that calculates compound interest but it's returning
wrong values for monthly compounding. Here's the code: [paste code]
Please identify the bug, explain what's wrong, and suggest a fix."

# Weak opening (no format guidance)
"Explain REST APIs."

# Better opening (scope and format)
"Give me a concise explanation of REST APIs for a developer who knows HTTP
but hasn't used REST before. Focus on the key constraints and why they matter.
Limit to 3–4 paragraphs — this is for a quick onboarding doc."
Starting With Context

For complex tasks, lead with context before the request. Claude performs better when it understands the situation before it tries to address the ask. 'I am building a mobile app for retail inventory management. I need a database schema for tracking stock levels across multiple store locations. Suggest a schema with appropriate tables, columns, and indexes.' This pattern — context first, then request — consistently produces higher-quality responses than leading with the request and hoping Claude infers the context.
Conversation Types You Can Start
Question and Answer

The simplest conversation type: you ask a question, Claude answers. Works well for factual queries, conceptual explanations, definitions, calculations, and any single-turn information need. After the answer, you can ask follow-up questions in the same conversation to go deeper.
Task Execution

You describe a task and Claude produces an artifact: a document, a code file, a translation, a summary. This type often requires a clear specification upfront — the more precisely you describe the desired output, the less back-and-forth revision is needed.
Iterative Collaboration

Many productive Claude sessions are iterative: Claude produces a draft, you provide feedback, Claude revises. This works well for writing, code review, and analysis. For iterative work, start with a clear initial request but expect to guide Claude through refinements across multiple turns rather than expecting a perfect output on the first message.
Analysis and Exploration

For research, analysis, or exploration of a topic, the conversation often unfolds organically — Claude produces an overview, you ask for expansion on specific points, and the session deepens progressively. Starting with 'Give me an overview of X and the key considerations' is a common and effective opener for this type.
Starting Fresh vs. Continuing an Existing Conversation
When to Start Fresh

Start a new conversation when the new topic is unrelated to a previous conversation, when you want a clean slate without prior assumptions, when a conversation has grown very long (approaching context limits), or when you want to try a different approach to the same problem without the history of the previous attempt influencing Claude.
When to Continue

Continue an existing conversation when the new question builds on previous context, when you want Claude to remember details from earlier in the session, or when you are working through a multi-step task that spans multiple turns. Long conversation history gives Claude more context but also consumes more of the context window — very long conversations may cause Claude to lose track of early details.
Navigating Past Conversations

All past conversations appear in the left sidebar, listed by date and with a truncated title based on the first message. You can search past conversations using the search bar if available. Clicking a past conversation reopens it so you can read the history and send new messages to continue it.
Interface Tips for a Smooth Start
Keyboard shortcut for new chat
— Shift+Option+N (Mac) or Shift+Alt+N (Windows) saves time when you open new chats frequently
Middle-click past conversations
— opens them in a new browser tab if you want to reference an old conversation while working in a new one
Use conversation titles
— Claude.ai automatically names conversations based on the first message; rename them for important conversations to find them later
Pin important conversations
— some interface versions allow pinning to keep frequently accessed conversations at the top of the list
Use projects for recurring contexts
— starting inside a project rather than a blank chat gives Claude better context from the first message
Common New-Chat Mistakes
Starting Without Enough Context

Many users start a chat with a single noun or verb — 'Python', 'email', 'marketing plan' — and expect Claude to know what to do. Claude can make assumptions and produce something, but the output may not match the need. Provide enough context in the first message that Claude could reproduce your mental image of the desired output.
Forgetting to Choose the Right Project

Starting a conversation outside a project when a relevant project exists means Claude lacks access to the project's instructions and knowledge. The conversation produces lower-quality, less consistent responses than it would inside the project. Before starting a conversation on a domain where you have set up a project, always navigate to that project first.
Sending Incremental Information Over Multiple Messages

Some users send context in fragments: first message is 'I have a Python problem', second is 'here is the code', third is 'this is the error'. This forces Claude to hold a developing picture across messages and can produce premature or wrong initial responses. Instead, write the full context — code, error, constraint, desired outcome — in a single opening message when possible.
Starting Conversations from Other Surfaces
Claude Desktop App

The Claude desktop app (available for macOS and Windows) provides a standalone application experience. New chats are started from the home screen or from a new chat button in the sidebar. The desktop app integrates with MCP servers for richer context capabilities, and a global keyboard shortcut can open Claude from anywhere on the desktop.
Claude Mobile App

The mobile app provides the same core conversation capability on iOS and Android. Voice input is available on mobile for hands-free messaging. New chats are started from the compose button. The mobile app syncs conversation history with the web interface so conversations started on one device continue seamlessly on another.
Third-Party Integrations

Claude is embedded in various third-party applications (see the Claude in Apps sub-subtopic). Starting a conversation in a third-party integration typically means invoking Claude through that application's interface — a slash command in Slack, a keyboard shortcut in VS Code, a button in Notion. The new conversation mechanism depends entirely on the integration.
Setting Expectations at the Start of a Conversation

The first message in a conversation does more than just pose a question — it establishes what kind of assistant Claude should be for the entire session, what depth is expected, what format is preferred, and what the user considers a satisfactory answer. Investing 30–60 extra seconds in a thorough opening message often saves multiple rounds of correction and revision.
Specifying Output Length

Claude calibrates its response length to what it infers from the prompt. A one-sentence question often receives a paragraph response; a detailed multi-sentence question often receives a multi-page response. If you have a specific length expectation that differs from what Claude might infer, state it explicitly: 'Give me a concise answer in 2–3 sentences', or 'Write a thorough explanation; I have time to read it'.
Specifying Depth and Technical Level

Depth guidance prevents responses that are either too elementary or too expert for the intended audience. 'Explain this as if I am a senior developer who knows Python but has not used async/await before' calibrates depth and assumed knowledge precisely. 'Give me the executive summary version for a non-technical stakeholder' does the same in the other direction.
Indicating What You Do Not Need

Negative constraints in the opening message prevent Claude from adding content you know you don't need: 'Don't include an introduction or conclusion, just the list'; 'Skip historical background, I only need current best practices'; 'No code examples, I need the conceptual explanation only'. These constraints are respected reliably and save editing time on the output.
Conversation Opening Patterns for Common Tasks
The Context-Request Pattern

Context: I'm building a REST API for an e-commerce platform using FastAPI.
The API needs to handle authentication, product catalog, cart, and checkout.
I'm using PostgreSQL and Redis.

Request: Design the database schema for the cart and checkout functionality.
Include the relationships with the existing users and products tables.
Show the SQL DDL.
The Sample-and-Transform Pattern

Here is a sample of the data format I need to transform:
[paste sample]

Transform it to this output format:
[describe or paste example output format]

Write a Python function that performs this transformation.
Handle edge cases for missing or null fields.
The Annotated-Example Pattern

Here is an example of the kind of writing I want:
[paste example]

Now write a similar piece about [new topic], following the same:
- Tone and register
- Paragraph length and structure
- Use of examples and analogies
- Level of technical detail
The Constraint-First Pattern

Constraints:
- Must work in Python 3.9+ (no walrus operator, no structural pattern matching)
- Cannot use third-party libraries
- Must handle Unicode input correctly
- Should run in O(n log n) time or better

Task: Write a function that [description].

Include type annotations and a brief docstring.
Using Slash Commands and Shortcuts

Claude.ai supports slash commands (prefixed with /) for quick actions that would otherwise require typing a longer instruction. The available commands depend on your interface version and may include:
/summarize
— ask Claude to summarize the current conversation or attached content
/translate
— translate selected text to another language
/improve
— improve the writing of a selected block of text
/explain
— ask Claude to explain selected code or content
Slash commands are most useful for quick, repeated actions. For complex multi-part requests, explicit natural language instructions give more control than a slash command.
Voice Input for New Conversations

Claude.ai's mobile app supports voice input via the device's microphone. Tapping the microphone icon in the message input area activates speech-to-text, which transcribes your spoken words into the text input box. You can then review, edit if needed, and send the transcribed message.

Voice input is particularly useful for longer conversational questions where typing is slow or inconvenient. It is less suited for messages that include code, technical syntax, or precise formatting requirements, where voice transcription errors can introduce ambiguity.
Starting a Conversation After Uploading Files

When you know the conversation will center on a specific document or image, upload the file as part of the first message rather than starting with plain text and uploading in a second message. Including the file with the opening message lets you write your initial prompt in the context of the file, giving Claude the full picture from the first turn.

# File-first opening messages

[Attach: quarterly-report.pdf]
"This is our Q3 2025 financial report. Please:
1. Extract the three key performance metrics from the executive summary.
2. Compare the revenue figures to Q2 2024.
3. Identify any risks mentioned in the risk factors section."

[Attach: codebase-overview.png]
"This is an architecture diagram of our system. Identify any single points
of failure and suggest redundancy strategies for each."

[Attach: requirements.docx]
"Review these product requirements for clarity and completeness. Flag any
requirements that are ambiguous, contradictory, or missing acceptance criteria."
Reopening and Resuming Past Conversations
Navigating the Conversation List

Past conversations are listed in the left sidebar, most recent first. Conversations are automatically named based on the first message (or first few words of it). If you have many conversations, use the search function — available as a magnifying glass icon or a keyboard shortcut — to search for keywords from past conversations.
Continuing vs. Starting Fresh

When you reopen a past conversation, all prior messages are in context. Claude can refer to earlier parts of the conversation. However, if the conversation is very old or very long, the oldest messages may have scrolled out of Claude's active context window. If you need to refer to content from early in a long conversation, either quote the relevant text in your new message or start a fresh conversation and paste the relevant prior context.
Branching a Conversation

Claude.ai does not natively support conversation branching (creating a fork from a specific point to explore a different direction). The workaround is to start a new conversation and explicitly set the context from the prior conversation: 'Continuing from an earlier conversation: I was working on X with the constraints Y and Z. Now I want to try a different approach to the authentication piece...' This resets the context without losing the framing.
Conversation Etiquette With Claude
While Claude doesn't require formality, certain conversational practices consistently produce better results:
Be direct
— Claude responds to direct requests better than hints or indirect suggestions. 'Rewrite this paragraph to be more concise' works better than 'This paragraph could maybe be a bit shorter?'
Give feedback, not just corrections

— instead of just providing a correct version, explain what was wrong: 'That response was too technical for my audience; rewrite it for a non-developer.' The explanation helps Claude apply the feedback across subsequent responses.
Say when you are satisfied
— telling Claude 'That's exactly what I needed' lets it know the task is complete and prevents it from offering unnecessary alternatives or follow-ups.
Ask for revisions explicitly
— 'Revise the third paragraph to emphasize cost savings more' is more effective than 'Can you make it better?' Specific revision requests produce targeted improvements.
Key Takeaways
New conversations in Claude.ai are started via the New chat button in the sidebar, a keyboard shortcut (Shift+Option+N), or the compose button in the mobile app.

The most important choice at conversation start is whether to open the chat inside a relevant project or as a standalone conversation — projects provide persistent instructions and knowledge that significantly improve response quality for domain-specific tasks.
Before sending, choose the model if multiple are available; the default (usually Claude 3.5 or 3.7 Sonnet) works well for most tasks.
A strong opening message includes: what you want, relevant context, desired format, and any constraints — specific and complete rather than brief and vague.
Four common conversation types are Q&A, task execution, iterative collaboration, and open-ended exploration; each benefits from a different opening message style.
Start a new conversation when topics change significantly or when a conversation grows very long; continue an existing conversation when the new message builds on prior context.
On all surfaces — web, desktop, mobile, and third-party integrations — the same principles apply: clear context, right model, right project for the task.
How to Use This Feature Effectively

Getting the most out of Starting a New Chat requires understanding both its capabilities and its limitations. This section provides concrete guidance on using the feature to its full potential, with practical examples you can apply immediately.

The key to effective use of Starting a New Chat is to start with clear intentions. Know what outcome you're aiming for before you begin, and set up the feature in a way that supports that outcome. Many users find it helpful to experiment with different approaches to discover what works best for their specific workflow.
Define your goal clearly before you start — vague intentions lead to vague results
Use the feature consistently to build familiarity and identify patterns
Combine with other Claude features for maximum effectiveness
Review outputs critically and provide feedback through follow-up prompts
Document what works for your use case so you can replicate success
Share knowledge with teammates to build organizational competency
Use Cases and Applications

There are many valuable use cases for Starting a New Chat across different contexts. Understanding the range of applications helps you identify where this feature can add the most value to your workflow.
Professional productivity: Streamline repetitive tasks and focus on higher-value work
Research and analysis: Gather and synthesize information more efficiently
Content creation: Generate first drafts, outlines, and creative ideas
Learning and education: Explore new topics and get explanations of complex concepts
Problem-solving: Work through challenges with an AI thinking partner
Code assistance: Write, review, debug, and explain code
Communication: Draft, edit, and polish written communications
Integration with Your Workflow
Integrating Starting a New Chat into your existing workflow is usually straightforward, but there are several strategies that can make the transition smoother and ensure you get maximum value.

Start by identifying the specific tasks where you'll use this feature most. Then create a small experiment to test how it fits into your existing process. Adjust your workflow based on what you learn, and gradually expand usage as you build confidence.
Map out your current workflow before adding Claude to identify integration points
Start with one specific task to build confidence before expanding usage
Create templates or prompts that you can reuse across similar tasks
Set up a feedback loop to continuously improve your approach
Track time savings and quality improvements to build the business case
Configuration and Settings

Configuring Starting a New Chat correctly for your needs is important for getting consistent, high-quality results. While the defaults work well for most use cases, understanding the available options allows you to fine-tune the behavior.

Take time to explore the settings and options available. Each setting serves a specific purpose, and understanding what each one does gives you greater control over the output. Don't be afraid to experiment — you can always revert to defaults if needed.
Review all available settings before assuming defaults are optimal for your use case
Make changes incrementally so you can understand the impact of each adjustment
Save your preferred configurations for easy reuse across sessions
Test configuration changes with representative examples before relying on them in production
Document your configuration choices and the reasons behind them
Tips and Tricks

Experienced users of Starting a New Chat have discovered many tips and tricks that significantly improve their results. These insights often come from experimentation and aren't always obvious from the documentation.
Use specific, concrete language rather than vague requests for better results
Break complex tasks into smaller steps for more reliable outcomes
Provide context and examples when working on specialized or domain-specific tasks
Use follow-up prompts to refine and improve initial outputs
Combine this feature with other Claude capabilities for compound benefits
Learn from both successes and failures to continuously improve your approach
Share tips with your team to build collective expertise
Detailed Walkthrough

This walkthrough takes you through Starting a New Chat step by step, explaining not just what to do but why each step matters. Following along will build both your practical skills and your conceptual understanding.

One of the most common challenges people face with Starting a New Chat is understanding how all the pieces fit together. This walkthrough addresses that by building up from first principles, showing how each component contributes to the overall result.
Step 1: Understand the goal — what specific outcome are you trying to achieve?
Step 2: Gather prerequisites — ensure you have everything needed before starting
Step 3: Configure the basics — set up the minimal working configuration first
Step 4: Test the basics — verify the simple case works before adding complexity
Step 5: Add features incrementally — build up functionality one piece at a time
Step 6: Handle edge cases — ensure robust behavior for unusual inputs
Step 7: Optimize and polish — refine the implementation for production use
Real-World Scenarios
Understanding how Starting a New Chat applies to real-world scenarios helps bridge the gap between learning and application. These scenarios represent common situations you'll encounter in practice.

Scenario 1: A startup team wants to quickly build a proof-of-concept. They need to move fast, so they prioritize simplicity and speed over optimization. This is the perfect situation to use the minimal configuration and iterate.

Scenario 2: An enterprise with strict compliance requirements needs to implement this feature with full audit logging, access controls, and documentation. They'll spend more time upfront designing the architecture correctly.
Scenario 3: A solo developer building a personal project wants maximum flexibility and customization. They can afford to experiment more freely because the stakes are lower.
Match your approach to your context — startups and enterprises have different constraints
Document your decisions early, even in prototypes, to avoid losing context later
Build in testing and validation from the start, regardless of the project size
Consider future requirements, but don't over-engineer for hypothetical needs
Troubleshooting Common Issues
When working with Starting a New Chat, you'll likely encounter some common issues. Understanding these in advance saves significant time and frustration.
Issue 1 — Getting unexpected results: This is often caused by incomplete or ambiguous instructions. Review your input carefully, be more specific about what you want, and try providing examples.

Issue 2 — Performance is slower than expected: Check if you're using the right model for your use case, consider whether you can reduce the amount of context you're sending, and look for opportunities to cache results.
Issue 3 — Inconsistent outputs: Add more constraints and examples to your prompts, use structured output formats, and consider breaking complex tasks into simpler subtasks.
Log all inputs and outputs during development to diagnose issues
Compare against known-good examples when outputs seem wrong
Check the Anthropic documentation for recent changes that might affect behavior
Use the Claude.ai interface to test prompts before integrating via the API
Advanced Configuration
Once you've mastered the basics of Starting a New Chat, you can explore advanced configuration options that unlock additional capabilities.
Custom parameters: Adjust model parameters to fine-tune behavior for your specific use case
System prompts: Use system-level instructions to establish consistent behavior across interactions
Multi-turn conversations: Leverage conversation history to build context over multiple turns
Tool integration: Combine with Claude's tool use capabilities for more powerful applications
Caching strategies: Use prompt caching to reduce costs and latency for repeated requests
Streaming: Implement streaming responses for real-time user experiences
Batch processing: Process multiple requests efficiently using batch APIs
Summary

This section provided a comprehensive exploration of Starting a New Chat, from basic usage through advanced scenarios. The key takeaways are: start simple and iterate, match your approach to your context, and always test with representative examples. With these foundations in place, you're ready to apply these skills confidently in your own projects.
